threat_model: Mfidel substrate conformance can become unsafe if exact fidel sequences are normalized, decomposed, reinterpreted as smaller written units, treated through forbidden internal-letter modeling, or converted into lossy transliteration as canonical storage. The main risks are substrate drift across SDK and kernel implementations, validator overclaim of cross-runtime parity, hidden terminal closure claims, and accidental admission of tokenizer or runtime authority. The mitigation is a non-runtime receipt that preserves exact sequences, binds grid refs, denies normalization and decomposition paths, keeps cross-runtime parity AwaitingEvidence, and records receipt refs for audit.

required_checks.check: runtime parity and terminal closure authority remain false
redaction check confirms no secrets, raw credentials, or private payloads are serialized
policy bypass check confirms Unicode normalization, decomposition, forbidden internal-letter modeling, and fidel-splitting tokenization remain denied
receipt integrity check confirms receipt refs, evidence refs, fixture counts, and parity blockers cannot drift silently
audit visibility check confirms proof coverage matrix includes the Mfidel substrate conformance receipt surface

findings.mitigation: Schema and validator force normalization, decomposition, forbidden internal-letter modeling, lossy canonical transliteration, and fidel-splitting flags to false.

mitigations: preserve exact fidel sequences in every fixture
bind each fixture to f[row][col] refs within row 1 through 34 and column 1 through 8
deny Unicode normalization, decomposition, recomposition, forbidden internal-letter modeling, lossy canonical transliteration, and fidel-splitting tokenization
keep Python, TypeScript, and Rust parity as AwaitingEvidence until runtime fixtures are bound
deny live source reads and terminal closure in Foundation Mode
